title: SEGMENT 8 — June 26, 2000: the White House announcement and the brokered tie

lines.text: SEGMENT 8 — June 26, 2000: the White House announcement and the brokered tie
By the spring of 2000, the race between the public consortium and Celera had become politically unmanageable.
Both sides claimed they were nearly done with a draft.
Both sides were briefing journalists and lawmakers against each other.
The Wall Street Journal, the New York Times, Nature, and Science were running competing stories every week.
Inside Celera, the assembly team — led by Eugene Myers — was racing to integrate their shotgun reads with the public BAC scaffold.

Inside the public consortium, the assembly team — led by Jim Kent at UC Santa Cruz, who in a famous all-nighter wrote the GigAssembler program on a homemade Linux cluster in four weeks — was racing to integrate the BAC fragments into a draft.
Both sides knew the truth: neither side had a finished sequence.
Both had drafts.
Both drafts had gaps.
Both drafts disagreed with each other in many regions.

If one side declared victory first, the other side would dispute the claim, and the resulting fight would tarnish the entire achievement in the eyes of the public, Congress, the press, and the scientific community.
The solution was political.

Ari Patrinos, the head of the Department of Energy's genome program, hosted a series of quiet dinners at his home in suburban Maryland in the spring of 2000, with Francis Collins and Craig Venter as the guests.
Over wine and pizza, Patrinos brokered a truce.

Both sides would announce a working draft of the human genome simultaneously, jointly, at a White House event chaired by President Bill Clinton, with Prime Minister Tony Blair joining by satellite from 10 Downing Street.
The event happened on Monday, June 26, 2000, in the East Room of the White House.
Clinton, Blair, Collins, and Venter stood on a podium together.
Clinton's speech included one of the most quoted lines in the history of biology: "Today, we are learning the language in which God created life.
Collins announced that the public consortium had completed a working draft covering about ninety percent of the genome at four-to-five-fold coverage.
Venter announced that Celera had independently assembled a draft of similar coverage and would publish the details.
They shook hands.
The race, officially, was a tie.
What was actually announced that day was not a finished genome.
It was a draft — full of gaps, full of low-confidence regions, with the centromeres and telomeres entirely missing and substantial portions of the euchromatin still fragmentary.
The completion would not come until 2003, and even then with substantial caveats we will address.
But the June 2000 announcement was a real and important milestone, and the politics of joint announcement turned out to be wise.

It defused the rivalry just enough that both sides could publish their drafts as companion papers in February 2001 — the public draft in Nature on February 15, 2001, the Celera draft in Science on February 16, 2001 — without one side undercutting the other.
The two papers, taken together, were a turning point.
They made the front page of every major newspaper in the world.

They established beyond any question that the human genome could be sequenced, that the era of genomics had begun, and that the technical and political infrastructure for whole-genome biology was now in place.
There is an aftermath note worth telling.
Celera's business model — patenting genes and selling subscription database access — turned out not to work.
The pharmaceutical industry was unwilling to pay for genome data that was nearly identical to what was freely available from GenBank under the Bermuda Principles.
Celera as a sequencing company effectively collapsed within a few years of the 2001 publication.
Venter himself left Celera in 2002, in conflict with the company's board, and went on to found the J.
Craig Venter Institute, which did fascinating later work including the first synthetic bacterial genome in 2010.
The lesson, if you want a lesson, is that the open-data principle of the public consortium was not just morally satisfying — it was also strategically devastating to the for-profit alternative.
By making the data free, the public consortium removed the foundation of Celera's business.
Bermuda won.
